title: Lowy Poll 2026: Australia’s nuclear taboo is under scrutiny
gist: The 2026 Lowy Poll shows a security-anxious Australia — over half feel unsafe, only one in five trust the US leader, 35% think the US would not defend Australia (up from 25% seven years ago) — with the 'strongly against' an Australian nuclear weapon dropping ten points since 2022. The author argues a nuclear capability would make Australia less safe: it would substitute for (not complement) the US alliance, invite Chinese pre-emption, cost ~$21bn/year, hollow out conventional forces, and spur regional proliferation.

insights.claim: The 2026 Lowy Poll shows more than half of Australians feel unsafe, only one in five trust the leader of their most important ally to act responsibly, and 35% believe the US would not come to Australia's defence if attacked — up from 25% seven years ago.
insights.speaker: David Vallance
insights.so_what: Eroding trust in US extended deterrence (35% doubting defence) is the leading driver of nuclear-taboo softening; a sentiment shift with long-run defence-policy and AUKUS implications.
insights.locator: The 2026 Lowy Institute Poll reflects an Australia uneasy

insights.claim: The number of Australians 'strongly against' an Australian nuclear weapon has dropped ten points since 2022, reflecting eroding trust in the US nuclear umbrella much as occurred in the 1960s.
insights.speaker: David Vallance
insights.so_what: A measurable ten-point taboo erosion signals nuclear debate entering the Australian mainstream; track the trajectory as a proxy for alliance-confidence decay.
insights.quote: The number of Australians who are "strongly against" this notion has dropped ten points from 2022.
insights.locator: This is a nation anxious about its security

themes.theme: Why a bomb would reduce safety

insights.claim: An Australian nuclear weapon would be an alternative, not a complement, to the US alliance — the US nuclear umbrella has acted as a counter-proliferation tool, so Australia would only consider a program if trust in the alliance had disintegrated, likely following US drawdown and cession of regional leadership to China.
insights.speaker: David Vallance
insights.so_what: A nuclear Australia implies a broken US alliance, not an enhanced one; the two are mutually exclusive — reframes the AUKUS/extended-deterrence trade-off.
insights.quote: An Australian nuclear weapon would be an alternative, not a complement, to its alliance with the United States.
insights.locator: An Australian nuclear weapon would be an alternative

insights.claim: China would have an interest in preventing Australia from acquiring nuclear weapons, and given enhanced Chinese long-range strike capabilities any state seeking such weapons would find itself threatened by China's conventional weapons — as Iran's program demonstrates.
insights.speaker: David Vallance
insights.so_what: Pursuing weapons invites destabilising pre-emption during the vulnerable build phase; the Iran analogy quantifies the in-transit risk.
insights.locator: Under those circumstances, China would have an interest

insights.claim: The UK's Defence Nuclear Enterprise consumed 18% of its defence budget in FY2024-25 — £10.0 billion, nearly $21 billion — greater than the Royal Australian Navy's capabilities, sustainment and nuclear-submarine program combined, so Australia could not maintain the ADF as constituted and operate a credible independent deterrent.
insights.speaker: David Vallance
insights.so_what: The ~$21bn/year UK comparator quantifies the fiscal trade-off; a nuclear program would cannibalise conventional ADF capability — a concrete budget-allocation argument.
insights.quote: the United Kingdom's Defence Nuclear Enterprise (DNE) consumed 18% of its defence budget, or £10.0 billion
insights.locator: The most relevant comparison for Australia

insights.claim: A nuclear weapon's strategic effect would be fundamentally passive — it can only deter, not serve constabulary, diplomatic or military roles — so funding it would mean less conventional ADF investment, abrogating Australia's intent to shape its strategic circumstances, a departure from every defence policy since Federation.
insights.speaker: David Vallance
insights.so_what: Deterrence-only utility plus opportunity cost makes nuclear a poor fit for Australia's active-shaping doctrine; weighs against the case despite shifting public sentiment.
insights.locator: Yes, a nuclear program would deter an invasion

insights.claim: Nuclear weapons can deter major war but not limited conflict and may make peripheral conflict more likely (the stability-instability paradox), so a nuclear-armed but hollowed-out Australia would be worse equipped for likely real threats like unrest in Indonesia, Timor-Leste or Solomon Islands, and would likely spur regional proliferation.
insights.speaker: David Vallance
insights.so_what: Nuclear acquisition addresses an unlikely threat (invasion) while degrading capacity for likely regional contingencies and raising proliferation risk — a net-negative security calculus.
insights.quote: Nuclear weapons can deter major war but not limited conflict.
insights.locator: A nuclear weapon is also no guarantee of stability
